sentences.text: Yes.
Yes, I would like you to know that my father, beside being a carpenter and a carriage maker, he was the only one in the Berezne-- in all these small, little hamlets-- who was making matzo every year.
We had two ovens.
And we had all kinds of machinery, which he owned.
And he was only one.
Right after Purim, the, the Mashgiach used to come down, and make sure that everything was kosher.
And then, they used to make matzo for about 5,000 Jews around in Berezne.
Yep.
The only one.
He had a lot of people working for him.
He had people working for him.
And, and he-- he had Jewish people and Christian people working for him.
Mashgiach is a-- is a man-- is not a rabbi, but is some kind of orthodox Jew who is allowed to supervise the kashrut for-- kashrut for the before you make the matzo.
That means he's got to make sure that the tables were clean, the machines, the rolls, were clean because people have to eat this.
I remember, since Purim to Pesach, we used to sleep in two rooms because everything was taken up with the matzo baking.
And that's how he made a very good living-- by being a carpenter, being a carriage maker, and baking matzo.
Yes.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And a week before Pesach, I remember the [NON-ENGLISH] came down.
And they used to make the shmurah matzo, which is only eaten by rabbis.
Which it's not square, it's round.
It's made by hand.
And they do it themselves.
And nobody touched it.
Not one of my dad's help touched it.
The only thing, he was the only one who baked the matzo.
They used to put in, in an oven, about 10 matzos, on one long-- like you do, here, the pizzas.
The round one was about eight inches diameter-- the shmurah matzah.
And--
Ultra, yeah.
They used to keep the flour from one year to the other, in sacks, in the women's section of the shul.
That means the corn which was taken from the fields was supervised by the rabbi.
And the stones which was grind for the-- to make the flour was-- they had their own stones.
Everything had to be done--
I don't know.
That was the only place they could hang the thing so nobody could touch it and nothing could happen.
Well, that's what I remember when I was a kid because we always looked at the big sacks, and were afraid of--
Yeah.
We had to survive that time.
And we did.
It was 10 guys.
They gave us 10 rifles.
That's all.
And two pistols.
With children.
With women, children, men.
When you talk about older, to me, my dad was an old man when he was 52.
My mother was 46 when she died.
At that time, old people, to me, was 30, 40 years is old.
Between, I'd say, six years to 50 years-- 47 years, 50 years.
Well, when the Russian advanced to Ukraine again and the Germans started to run, we came out from the woods.
Yeah.
And we came out from the woods, and we came to Berezne, in 1944 in the spring.
To Berezne in the spring to the same ghetto where they used to be the ghetto for the Jews.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Back to the city, back to the whatever was left.
No more Germans.
Was Russians.
All Russians.
And we, we, we settled down.
Matter of fact, at that time, me and my brother were together with the three sisters, which is she's now his wife.
The name was Enya Gruber, and Reina Gruber, and Pesa Gruber.
And he married the pretty one-- Reina Gruber.
And we stayed there till the army-- till the government drafted-- took my brother to the army.
Russian.
Russian government took him in the army.
And then I stayed with them alone.
I used to work with a man who we supplied some food coming back from-- coming from Rovno, bring it to Berezne for the people.
Rovno.
Rovno.
Yeah, [INAUDIBLE] Rovno.
And we stayed there till we heard that the Yalta Declaration was that all the Jewish people who were Poles at that time, before the war-- and now they're Russians-- they can go back to Poland.
And that's how we applied to go back to Poland.
And they let us out.
Berezne.
Which it was Russia.
Right?
But they are going-- the Germans were still there in Germany, in part of Poland, were still there.
And I think what they had in mind to do is they're going to make Poland part of Poland, after the Yalta.
When they had a-- they-- the Polish people who fought with the Russians against the Germans-- which the generals were in England.
They were saved in England.
They had an agreement with the Russians.
After the war, they'll let all the Polish citizens back to-- Polish made-- when the war was going to be over, and Russia will stop, and then they'll make Poland a part of Poland.
Yes.
But Ukraine was still was going to be Russia.
The part where I was born was going to be Russia, up to, I don't know, Sluch, whatever.
So that's when I, and most of the Jewish people who were surviving the pogroms decided that we're going to go.
Because we heard that the Haganah is working already in the part of Poland which is going to be Poland.
So we figured we'll go down there, and see if we can go to Israel.
Yes.
Well, first of all, we didn't have no-- nothing to do in Berezne.
Everybody was killed.
We didn't have nothing in there.
And nothing bind me to stay in Berezne.
And after what happened, so we figured we'll go.
We'll see what can happen.
Because we knew that we have an aunt, uncle, cousins in America.
And we know that the Haganah is working to take some people out to Palestine.
And we figured that's the only way to go.
And that's what we did.
Oh, yes.
No.
My brother was-- yeah.
My brother was taken to the army.
My new sister-in-law, and the two sisters of hers.
Yeah.
In Chelyabinsk in Russia.
When my brother was in the hospital, and he send a letter to his wife because he got married in Berezne, right after when we came out from the woods.
Yeah.
Yes, you can.
You can--
Well, it took maybe three months, but it--
Well, we drove.
Yeah.
We take a train, and we went down.
Then we came to in Poland, in Galicia.
The war is not over yet.
But there's a lot of cars, trucks, Russian going-- Russians going.
Russians using trains.
You, you take anything you can.
Yeah.
And if you couldn't drive inside, you went on the roof, and you held on to the railing to the roof.
The Germans weren't there anymore.
The Russian army was already in Poland on the German-- in the beginning of the German-- old Germany.
And they were fighting there.
I would say between-- I would say 15 to 30 people.
Not that I know.
No.
They stayed.
We were near Krakow, near Czestochowa.
This is the old-- actually, they were right next to Germany.
And they were German sympathizer, most of the Polish people there.
But it was in the, I would say, southwest.
Because up there, that's the only place that we heard that the Jewish Palestine working to get them out from there.
Well, there was-- there was a few homes, which they-- which you go in, in there, and they gave you a place to sleep.
The, the Haganah already there, and they gave you food.
And we stayed there for close to four weeks.
And you meet up with so many people from kinds of German concentration camps, and Russian, people who run away from the army, and Polish people.
You meet up with so many people that it was like a, a smorgasbord from people.
And then, you listen to stories.
You don't know where to go.
You don't know what to do.
And I'm only, what?
At that time, 16 years old, 17 years old?
And, and all of a sudden the Poles started another pogrom on the Jews while we were there.
Now, where were we exactly, at this time?
That was in-- where's my paper here?
It's Czestochowa in Gleiwitz.
It's part of Poland which is next to the German border.
This is in 1944 when the Russian were on the march.
And they already took over Poland.
And they are fighting-- they were still fighting in Germany.
And the Americans were fighting.
They already had the second front open.
They were fighting.
They were coming down from, from the, the-- from--
Normandy, yeah.
By the Poles, against Jews.
There was a stabbing.
I think they killed-- but I ran away with my-- a few of us ran away.
And the next few days--
I don't know.
But the next few weeks we decided that we're going to have to go farther south.
And we went into that-- to the place where there was supposed to be an office.
And we gave our names that we want to go to Palestine.
And after a week or so, they gave us-- they gave us a lecture that you're going to be, as Greek refugees, going back to Greece from the concentration camp in Poland.
And they gave us each false names, which I don't even remember the name.
But the only thing I remember is when they told us if you see a Russian soldier in the train, just say [NON-ENGLISH].
And don't speak any Russian, Russian or Jewish.
And they--
Greek.
And at that time, it was in the night.
And about 3:00 in the morning, we went-- we came with a train to Pilzno, Czechoslovakia.
And then, when we went off the train and then we saw the first-- The train-- when we came from Poland, they didn't ask us anything.
I mean the Polish-- the Russian soldiers were on the train, but they didn't bother us.
And when we came to Pilzno, there was already the American zone.
And we saw the first black people as Americans.
And we stayed in, in, in Pilzno about two weeks in makeshift buildings, which they gave us.
No, 1944. '44 I left.
Well, yeah, '44.
The Americans came-- at that time, maybe they came from Italy.
They came from-- yeah.
And I-- it could be maybe the later part because it was chilly at night.
And we stayed there for a few weeks in Pilzno.
And I remember going to a steam bath to Prague.
I never saw a steam bath like this in my life.
And--
With lice?
Yeah.
Plenty of lice.
We couldn't help ourselves.
What you used to do, you used to take the shirt and turn over on the other side and just go throw on the fire to burn them off.
But the eggs were still there.
And, and we came-- finally, we came to our destination that's where-- which I stayed in, in Linz, by the name of Bindermichl--
Bindermichl.
--which used to be-- used to be the barracks-- not the barracks, the homes for the Germans, German officers.
The families used to live there.
That's where they, they took, and they made a DP camp for the people in Linz-Bindermichl.
That was Austria.
At that time, we felt like we owned the city because with-- there was nothing to afraid already there.
So we used to run around.
And they, they were like the beaten people.
They weren't too demanding.
Wherever we went, nobody touched us at that time.
Very nicely.
We had a few friends.
But we were a gang of-- gang about eight people, 10 people, which we used to [NON-ENGLISH].
That means take revenge whatever we could because the Austrians were worse than the Germans.
And we survived.
About this?
We used to-- first of all we bought an old car, all of us, by making a few bucks in the camp by selling cigarettes-- American cigarettes--
This is still during the war?
This is during the war, yes.
This is 1940-- beginning of 1945.
And I remember going to Italy-- taking a train to Villach.
And from Villach you walked.
We used to exchange money and bring it back.
And this is how I made a living.
I'll go to Budapest and come back to Austria.
You didn't have papers at that time.
The only thing I had is that I live in-- that my name is Gershkowitz, and I live in the DP camp in Bindermichl.
That's all.
And the thing was staatenlos.
That means I have-- didn't have no state.
And we had a good time.
We-- what can you do?
It's been a long time, and you know, you got to live.
Have you-- while you were in Bindermichl, did you learn anything about what was going on in Poland with the Jewish people?
Yes, we did.
We did.
And matter of fact, the first thing we, we had to do is find out if they know about me in America because I have an aunt with a family and an uncle with a family.
And finally, they find out that we're there.
And they send us papers to come to America.
This was in 1945.
They-- because the UNRRA used to send lists of names to America from the survivors.
And that's how my family here find out that we were alive.
And then they started to work with the consulate here to see if they can take us out because we were ready to go to Israel.
I was-- to Palestine, at that time.
We were on the list to go to Palestine till we got papers that we could go to America.
Sure because we had already people with the numbers in our camps.
And they used to tell us, from different-- like in Latvia.
In, in, in the-- in Poland-- in Poland, which, which they already-- the Russians came in, and they let out all.
Then we-- they used to send them any place.
And Linz was a very, very big DP camp.
Yeah.
I think they had over 8,000 people.
It was a whole city.
And we met people from-- a matter of fact, a very good friend of my brother's was in concentration camp.
He had the numbers.
And he told us everything, what's going on.
But we knew-- in the partisans, we knew some of it, what's going on already.
The end of the war-- yes.
The end of the German war.
They made us-- we were-- like I said before, I worked with a man who-- we, we had a truck.
And we used to go to Rovno to bring a lot of food for these Berezne.
And while  we was in Rovno they said that the-- that we should all go to the square because Roosevelt died.
And they made a very big to-do at that time, April 12, 1945.
And-- and then they made us-- one time when we were there, they made us go to a square because they caught German collaborators-- Ukraine and Polish people-- and they hang them on the big square.
And they made all the people should come down and see it.
Well, at the time I wasn't frightening anymore.
Nope.
I wasn't frightened anymore.
After the war, yes.
Yes.
That was when we came from Russia to Poland.
At that time, we were in the Galicia.
That was a very anti-Semitic part of Poland-- Czestochowa.
And they still didn't think that the Jews will be survived.
And all of a sudden they see Jews.
So they had another pogrom.
This was in 1944.
Yeah.
OK.
